specification.topic-statement: The link-seam wrapper system exposes every external library call and selected internal call through a thin indirection layer that test builds can replace without modifying production code.

scope.in-scope: The MOCKABLE symbol attribute, the two-mode compilation strategy (inline in release, weak symbol in debug), the aggregation header that assembles all wrapper families into a single include, and the general contract that every wrapped call is a transparent passthrough unless a test overrides it.
scope.boundary: The specific functions wrapped in each family (HTTP client, HTML parsing, internal application functions, POSIX, standard library, JSON, talloc) are documented separately. This specification covers only the mechanism that applies uniformly to all of them.

Tests replace wrapped functions by providing a strong-symbol definition of the same name. The linker resolves the strong definition over the weak one. The test framework conventions for doing this are outside scope.

field.name: release build
field.note: expands to 'static inline'; definition lives in the header; call is inlined at the call site with zero overhead

field.name: debug/test build
field.note: expands to '__attribute__((weak))'; definition lives in the .c file; the symbol is a weak global that any translation unit can override

behavior.release-mode-dispatch: In release builds (NDEBUG defined), each wrapper is defined as a static inline function directly in its header file. The compiler inlines the call at every use site, producing identical machine code to calling the underlying function directly. No wrapper symbol appears in the object file or binary. No runtime indirection exists.
behavior.debug-mode-dispatch: In debug and test builds (NDEBUG not defined), each wrapper is declared as a weak symbol in the header and defined as a weak symbol in a corresponding .c file. The weak definition calls through to the underlying function unchanged. Any translation unit may provide a strong-symbol definition of the same name; the linker selects the strong definition over the weak one, replacing the call everywhere in the binary without source modification.
behavior.transparent-passthrough: When no test override is present, every wrapper forwards all arguments to the underlying function and returns its result unchanged. Arguments are not validated, logged, or transformed. The wrapper is behaviorally invisible.
behavior.aggregation-header:
A single umbrella header includes all wrapper family headers in one directive. Code that needs access to the full set of wrapped symbols includes only this umbrella. The umbrella itself contains no wrapper definitions; it is purely compositional.
The families included are: talloc allocation, JSON library, HTTP client (curl), POSIX system calls, standard library, and internal application functions.
The HTML parsing wrappers are not included in the umbrella header. Code that needs them must include the HTML wrapper header directly. This is the only wrapper family omitted from the umbrella.
behavior.variadic-limitation:
Some underlying functions are variadic (accept variable argument lists). Wrapping variadic functions requires special handling.
In release builds, variadic functions that cannot be expressed as inline functions are wrapped using C preprocessor macros that expand directly to the underlying call, bypassing the wrapper mechanism entirely. These macros cannot be overridden by tests.
In debug builds, variadic wrappers are defined as proper functions. They extract a single generic pointer argument from the variadic list and forward it to the underlying function. This means only the first variadic argument is forwarded; the wrapper does not support multiple variadic arguments.
The debug-mode variadic wrappers extract variadic arguments as a raw void pointer (va_arg with void* type) regardless of the actual type the underlying function expects. For integer or enum arguments this works by coincidence on platforms where pointer and integer registers align, but is not formally correct C.
behavior.lcov-exclusion: All wrapper definitions in debug/test builds are marked for exclusion from line coverage analysis. Because these functions only delegate to the real implementation, covering them adds no meaningful assurance and would inflate coverage accounting. Release builds have no symbols to exclude.
